title: Morris Worm Investigation
description: A safe cyber range scenario inspired by the Morris Worm. Trainees observe simulated propagation artifacts and investigate processes, network ports, and file timestamps.
prerequisites: Understanding of processes and TCP ports
objectives: Identify listening ports used by a worm-like process
Map a process to a TCP listener
Inspect filesystem artifacts created during a simulated infection
Create a simple forensic timeline from file timestamps

levels.title: Scenario briefing
levels.content: A simplified Morris-worm-like simulator has been deployed in this sandbox. The simulator is safe: it does not exploit real vulnerabilities or self-propagate outside the lab. Your task is to observe and investigate the resulting artifacts.

levels.title: Reconnaissance
levels.content: From the analyst machine, identify which victim machines expose TCP port 9999. Useful commands: nmap, ss, netstat.

levels.title: Process investigation
levels.content: Find the process on a victim that is listening on TCP port 9999. Submit the script name.
hints: Use ss -ltnp or netstat -tulpn on a victim.
The process is started by a systemd service.

levels.title: Artifact investigation
levels.content: Run the safe simulator from the attacker machine, then inspect /opt/morris on a victim. Which artifact file is created after the simulated infection signal?

levels.title: Timeline
levels.content: Use stat on /opt/morris/badfile and /opt/morris/infection.log. Which command is used to display mtime, ctime, and atime?
hints: ls -l is useful, but one command gives all three timestamps clearly.

levels.title: Final flag
levels.content: After completing the investigation, read /root/flag.txt on victim3 and submit the flag.
hints: You may need sudo/root access depending on the sandbox user configuration.
